title: Early-Game Tea Saplings: Fast Cash Without Building a Whole Farm Around Them
summary: Use Tea Saplings as a controlled early-game cash bridge: befriend Caroline, convert spare forage resources into gold, and stop before the route steals materials from your farm setup.
bestFor: Players who need early Spring cash before sprinklers, animals, or artisan machines are online
Players who can reach Caroline's 2-heart event without ignoring planting, fishing, or mining progress
Players with spare forage, fiber, and wood who want money now more than future crafting flexibility
whyItWorks: Tea Saplings turn mixed early resources into a predictable sale item, which helps when the farm needs a short-term cash push.
The route does not require a large watered field, so it fits players who are still limited by energy and tool quality.
Its value is strongest as a bridge: it helps fund the next system, then becomes less important once crops, animals, or machines take over.
setupSteps: Start building Caroline friendship early, because the route only matters after her 2-heart event unlocks the recipe by mail.
Before selling forage, decide which items should become Wild Seeds and which can be shipped without blocking the sapling plan.
Keep enough fiber and wood for basic farm needs first; craft saplings only from resources that will not delay chests, buildings, or tool plans.
Craft and sell a small batch when a specific purchase is waiting, such as seeds, a backpack, or an upgrade fee.
Plant only the Tea Bushes you actually want to maintain; sell the rest if the goal is immediate cash.
Stop expanding the route once watering capacity, animals, kegs, jars, or stronger crop plans give you a better use for time and resources.

profitEstimates.label: Single sapling cash-out
profitEstimates.assumption: Current 1.6 wiki value for selling one crafted Tea Sapling; forage, fiber, and wood opportunity costs are not subtracted.
profitEstimates.value: 250g per Tea Sapling, before valuing the materials you used.

profitEstimates.label: Small early batch
profitEstimates.assumption: Derived estimate for 20 crafted Tea Saplings sold at the listed value; useful as a planning example, not a full-route net-profit model.
profitEstimates.value: About 5,000g before material costs, friendship time, and alternative uses for forage are considered.

caveats: Older advice may assume the pre-1.6 500g sell price, so use current values when judging the route.
The route fails if it drains fiber, wood, or seasonal forage that you needed for farm construction and seed planning.
It is weak as a permanent money plan because it does not scale like sprinklers, animals, or artisan processing.
If you cannot reach Caroline's 2-heart event early, treat this as a later bonus rather than a first-week plan.
articleAngles: Use Tea Saplings to solve a short cash problem, not to define the whole farm economy.
The decision is whether immediate gold is worth more than keeping forage, fiber, and wood flexible.
The 1.6 price change makes the route a controlled bridge instead of an exploit-style answer.
A good batch has a purpose: fund the next upgrade, then move on.
notes: Wiki/database source tier. Core mechanics and the 1.6 sell-price context are source-backed; batch value is derived from the listed sale value and should stay framed as a planning estimate rather than guaranteed net profit.

title: Spring Year 1 Strawberries: Planning the Egg Festival Without Starving Your Farm
summary: Plan Strawberries around the Egg Festival as a cash-and-labor decision: bring enough gold to matter, buy before the Egg Hunt, and avoid planting more than you can water.
bestFor: Players who want a Spring Year 1 crop spike without overloading their morning routine
Players who can prepare a watered plot immediately after the Egg Festival
Players deciding how much festival money can become seeds without delaying backpack, tool, or Summer plans
whyItWorks: Strawberries are bought at a fixed festival window, so preparation before Spring 13 determines how useful the route becomes.
Because they regrow, seeds planted right after the festival can still create multiple harvest opportunities in the same Spring.
The route rewards controlled spending: enough seeds to make the festival count, but not so many that watering consumes the rest of the season.
setupSteps: Before Spring 13, set a seed budget that leaves gold for at least one non-crop priority, such as a backpack, tool upgrade, or Summer seed reserve.
Clear the future Strawberry area before the festival so the evening planting window is realistic.
At the Egg Festival, buy seeds before starting the Egg Hunt; once the event ends, the shop window is gone.
Plant and water the seeds the same evening if energy, time, and field layout allow it.
Keep the field size matched to your watering capacity until sprinklers reduce the daily labor cost.
After the first harvest, use the income to stabilize the farm instead of automatically expanding every crop line.

profitEstimates.label: Per-crop base value
profitEstimates.assumption: Wiki-listed base Strawberry sell price, before quality, Tiller, processing, or extra berry chance.
profitEstimates.value: 120g per base-quality Strawberry.

profitEstimates.label: Festival planting model
profitEstimates.assumption: Derived planning model using the listed growth and regrowth behavior when seeds are planted after the Egg Festival; output varies with watering, quality, and harvest rolls.
profitEstimates.value: Treat Spring Year 1 Strawberries as a limited multi-harvest cash crop, not a perfect-season income guarantee.

caveats: This route fails if you start the Egg Hunt before buying seeds.
Overbuying creates energy pressure and can delay mining, fishing, tool upgrades, or Community Center progress.
If you cannot plant the same evening, the route still works but loses part of the time advantage.
Saving some seeds for later-year planning is a separate decision and should not be mixed with the Year 1 cashflow goal.
articleAngles: The Strawberry question is not 'buy or skip'; it is how much cash and watering time you can safely commit.
A smaller field you water every day beats a huge field that makes the rest of Spring collapse.
Festival crops work best when the player prepares the field before the event, not after it.
Use Strawberries to create momentum for Summer, not to starve every other upgrade.
notes: Wiki/database source tier. Seed timing, crop timing, and base value are source-backed; exact Spring return remains route-dependent because watering, planting time, quality, and player priorities vary.

title: Summer Blueberry Cashflow: Stable Regrowth Before Your Artisan Setup Is Ready
summary: Use Blueberries as a Summer cashflow stabilizer: plant early, keep the field maintainable, and let repeated harvests fund sprinklers, machines, and Fall preparation.
bestFor: Players entering Summer Year 1 with a manageable watering plan or early sprinklers
Players who need recurring income instead of waiting for one large payout
Players building toward Fall seeds, tool upgrades, jars, kegs, or animal infrastructure
whyItWorks: A single Blueberry seed purchase can pay across repeated Summer harvests, which keeps cash moving through the season.
Multiple berries per harvest create frequent shipping decisions even before artisan machines are ready.
The crop fits gradual infrastructure growth: every harvest can fund the next sprinkler, machine, or seasonal goal.
setupSteps: On Summer 1, choose a Blueberry field size that your current watering setup can support without consuming the whole morning.
Plant early enough to benefit from the crop's regrowth rhythm across the season.
Use the first harvests for infrastructure, not just more storage: sprinklers, tool upgrades, jars, kegs, and Fall seed money all matter.
Ship part of the crop when you need cash now; process only the amount your actual machines can handle.
Watch storage pressure after each harvest and reduce backlog before it blocks the next farm decision.
Treat Blueberries as a Summer stabilizer, then reassess whether Fall Cranberries, pumpkins, animals, or machines are the next better step.

profitEstimates.label: Base crop reference
profitEstimates.assumption: Wiki-listed Blueberry mechanics: Summer crop with regrowth and multiple berries per harvest; values vary by quality and profession.
profitEstimates.value: Use Blueberries as recurring cashflow rather than a fixed daily-profit promise.

profitEstimates.label: Processed surplus model
profitEstimates.assumption: Derived route advice for a high-yield fruit when Preserves Jars or other machines are available; machine count and cash timing decide how much should be processed.
profitEstimates.value: Process surplus only when it does not trap too much Summer cash in storage.

caveats: The route breaks down when the field is larger than your watering or sprinkler capacity.
Blueberries can create storage piles that look profitable but delay cash if machines are too limited.
This is a reliable cashflow route, not a claim that Blueberries beat every Summer option.
If you need a single large purchase immediately, a slower recurring crop may not solve the timing problem.
articleAngles: Blueberries are valuable because they smooth Summer income while the farm is still being built.
The main mistake is scaling crop count faster than watering, processing, and storage systems.
Recurring harvests should pay for future capacity, not create a pile of unprocessed fruit.
The right Blueberry field is the one that leaves time for mining, fishing, and upgrade progress.
notes: Wiki/database source tier. Crop mechanics are source-backed; route advice is original decision guidance based on watering capacity, cashflow timing, and processing bottlenecks.

title: Fall Cranberry Scaling: Big Seasonal Cash Without Losing Control of the Farm
summary: Use Cranberries as a Fall scaling crop only if your farm can handle repeated harvests: plant early, size the field around sprinklers, and keep cash moving toward winter infrastructure.
bestFor: Players entering Fall with enough seed money for a meaningful recurring crop plan
Players with sprinklers or a dependable watering routine
Players who want Fall income to fund winter upgrades, buildings, animals, or artisan machines
whyItWorks: Cranberries are a regrowing Fall crop, so planting early can turn one seed purchase into repeated seasonal income.
Repeated harvests help fund multiple upgrades instead of waiting for one large payout.
The route tests farm capacity: it performs well when watering, harvesting, and processing are planned before Fall 1.
setupSteps: Before Fall 1, decide how much morning labor your crop field can take after animals, mining, fishing, and tool plans are considered.
Match the Cranberry field to sprinkler coverage first; expand manually watered tiles only if the routine stays realistic.
Reserve part of your gold for non-crop Fall priorities instead of spending every coin on seeds.
Plant early so the regrowth cycle has time to matter across the season.
Plan harvest handling before the first large pickup: decide what gets shipped immediately and what enters jars or other machines.
Use the resulting cash for winter infrastructure rather than letting all value sit in a chest.

profitEstimates.label: Seasonal scaling model
profitEstimates.assumption: Derived from Cranberries being a regrowing Fall crop; output depends on planting date, watering, quality, field size, and extra cranberry mechanics.
profitEstimates.value: Treat Cranberries as a recurring Fall cash engine whose final return is controlled by field size and labor capacity.

profitEstimates.label: Processing decision
profitEstimates.assumption: Preserves Jar mechanics can improve low-value fruit, but only the amount your machine network can process before cash is needed should be held back.
profitEstimates.value: Ship enough to fund the season; process only the portion your jars can clear without creating a long backlog.

caveats: A large Cranberry field can become a daily chore if sprinklers are not ready.
Processing every berry is often unrealistic for a new farm with few jars.
The route is weaker if planted late or if harvest days repeatedly conflict with other priorities.
This guide assumes normal season rules, not modded calendars or economy changes.
articleAngles: Cranberries are a scaling discipline test: the right field size matters more than the biggest field size.
Fall income should build winter capacity, not just inflate a crop backlog.
Repeated harvests are useful only when the farm keeps turning them into decisions and upgrades.
The strongest version of the route starts with sprinkler planning before seed buying.
notes: Wiki/database source tier. Crop and processing mechanics are source-backed; profitability remains conditional because field size, quality, harvest timing, and machine count vary by save.

title: Ancient Fruit Wine and Keg Scaling: Build the Engine Before You Chase Bigger Numbers
summary: Build Ancient Fruit Wine as a machine-balanced engine: secure permanent growing space, expand seed stock patiently, and grow keg capacity before harvests overwhelm storage.
bestFor: Players with Greenhouse or Ginger Island farming space
Players who want steady weekly artisan income instead of constant replanting
Players ready to plan around oak resin, keg crafting, machine placement, and harvest rhythm
whyItWorks: Ancient Fruit becomes strong after the slow startup because the crop regrows on a predictable weekly rhythm.
Kegs turn the fruit into a higher-value artisan product, so machine capacity becomes the real scaling constraint.
Permanent growing space removes seasonal interruptions and makes the weekly routine easier to repeat.
setupSteps: Do not treat Ancient Fruit as your main engine until you have permanent or long-term growing space ready.
Expand seed stock gradually and keep expectations modest while the first plants mature.
Build kegs during the seed-expansion phase so harvests do not sit unprocessed for weeks.
Track the weekly rhythm: harvest, reload machines, and keep the process simple enough to repeat.
Use casks as a limited finishing bonus after the wine engine works; do not let cask space define the whole route.
If fruit inventory rises faster than keg capacity, craft more machines or reduce expansion pressure before chasing bigger fields.

profitEstimates.label: Per-fruit processing reference
profitEstimates.assumption: Wiki-listed Ancient Fruit and Wine values before farm layout, keg count, profession choice, and seed-expansion speed are applied.
profitEstimates.value: Ancient Fruit Wine is a high-value processed good, but exact route profit depends on how many kegs can run consistently.

profitEstimates.label: Mature engine model
profitEstimates.assumption: Derived planning model for a permanent field with enough kegs to process the weekly harvest; no full-farm net-profit guarantee and no cask-aging assumption.
profitEstimates.value: Estimate by matching weekly harvest volume to working keg capacity, then adjust for profession, missed cycles, and layout downtime.

caveats: Ancient Fruit starts slowly, so it is poor for players who need immediate cash this week.
Kegs compete for oak resin and other materials; machine supply often lags behind crop expansion.
Aging every bottle in casks is space-limited and should be treated as a bonus, not the normal engine rate.
The route loses value if the player dislikes weekly machine routines or lets fruit pile up without processing.
articleAngles: Ancient Fruit Wine is a balanced-system problem: crop space, seed stock, kegs, and weekly routine must mature together.
The best practical move is to build machines while the crop expands, not after harvests start piling up.
Weekly rhythm is the advantage; use it to make the farm predictable.
Do not compare Ancient Fruit to burst crops without considering startup time and machine readiness.
notes: Wiki/database source tier. Crop and wine mechanics are source-backed; route strength remains derived because keg count, profession choice, seed expansion speed, permanent space, and player routine vary by farm.

title: Starfruit Wine Burst Profit: When a Big Batch Beats a Slow Engine
summary: Use Starfruit Wine as a planned burst-income route when you already have seed access, field capacity, and kegs ready; otherwise the batch can become expensive storage.
bestFor: Players with Desert access and enough cash to buy Starfruit Seeds without draining the farm
Players who want large batch payouts rather than a slow seed-expansion engine
Players with kegs ready before harvest day, not after it
whyItWorks: Starfruit has a high base value, so processing it into wine creates a strong batch payout when the setup is ready.
The route converts upfront cash into a later artisan cash event, which can fund major upgrades or a transition into another engine.
Unlike Ancient Fruit, Starfruit is a deliberate replanting strategy, so it rewards clear timing and batch planning.
setupSteps: Confirm Starfruit Seed access and calculate whether buying the batch still leaves enough gold for the rest of the week.
Choose a growing space where watering, sprinklers, or permanent crop infrastructure can support the full batch.
Prepare keg capacity before the harvest arrives so the crop does not sit in a chest for too long.
Replant only when the next seed purchase, watering plan, and machine queue still fit your farm's tempo.
Use the finished wine payout for a defined goal, such as machines, buildings, or the next permanent engine.
If seed cost or keg backlog feels tight, shrink the batch instead of forcing the route.

profitEstimates.label: Per-bottle reference
profitEstimates.assumption: Wiki-listed Starfruit and Wine mechanics; net value still depends on seed cost, profession, quality handling, and processing backlog.
profitEstimates.value: Starfruit Wine is a high-value artisan product, but planning should subtract seed cost and time before judging the route.

profitEstimates.label: Burst batch model
profitEstimates.assumption: Derived route model for a planned Starfruit field with enough kegs available when the harvest arrives.
profitEstimates.value: Strong burst profit when seed cost is affordable and machine capacity is already built; weaker when the crop waits in storage.

caveats: The route fails if the seed purchase delays essential upgrades or leaves the farm cash-starved.
A large batch without enough kegs becomes a storage problem, not immediate profit.
Replanting labor and seed cost make this different from regrowing Ancient Fruit.
It is not a universal replacement for other engines; it solves a different cash-timing problem.
articleAngles: Starfruit Wine is planned burst income: cash goes in first, a larger payout comes later.
The key question is whether seeds, field space, and kegs are ready at the same time.
Batch size should follow your machine queue, not your ambition.
Use Starfruit to fund the next system instead of letting it create a long processing backlog.
notes: Wiki/database source tier. Item and processing mechanics are source-backed; route value is conditional on seed access, profession choice, replanting labor, field capacity, and keg count.

title: Pig, Truffle, and Truffle Oil Economy: Sell Direct or Process?
summary: Use pigs as a barn-income route when you can afford the setup and maintain outdoor access; decide between direct Truffle sales and Truffle Oil based on labor, professions, and routine.
bestFor: Players who want strong animal income instead of expanding another crop field
Players with Deluxe Barn access, feeding capacity, and outdoor space for pigs
Players comparing lower-friction Truffle sales against extra Oil Maker handling
whyItWorks: Fed mature pigs can find Truffles outdoors under valid conditions, turning barn investment into repeated collection income.
Truffles can be valuable as direct sales, especially when profession choices affect quality.
Oil Makers can add value in some setups, but the route only improves if the extra machine work fits your daily routine.
setupSteps: Upgrade to a Deluxe Barn only when the upfront building, animal, and feeding costs will not freeze the rest of the farm.
Give pigs clean outdoor space so Truffles are easy to find and the area does not become cluttered.
Keep pigs fed and collect Truffles regularly to maintain a readable routine.
Compare your current direct-sale value against Truffle Oil value before crafting a large Oil Maker network.
Process only the Truffles that fit your machine capacity; sell direct when time, profession setup, or simplicity matters more.
Before winter, remember that pig income changes sharply and plan another cash source for the season.

profitEstimates.label: Direct sale reference
profitEstimates.assumption: Wiki-listed Truffle values and pig production constraints; actual Truffle count depends on outdoor access, weather, season, friendship, and collection routine.
profitEstimates.value: Direct Truffle income can be high, especially when quality is improved by profession choices.

profitEstimates.label: Processing decision model
profitEstimates.assumption: Derived comparison between selling Truffles and using Oil Makers; practical value depends on profession setup, machine count, and daily labor tolerance.
profitEstimates.value: Process when the Oil Maker gain is worth the extra handling; sell direct when speed and routine simplicity matter more.

caveats: Pigs need valid outdoor conditions and do not find Truffles in winter.
Rain, blocked tiles, clutter, and missed collection can reduce practical output.
The route has a large upfront cost, so it is poor when the farm still needs basic tools, crops, or building foundations.
Yearly profit claims need farm-specific tracking and should not be treated as guaranteed.
articleAngles: The Truffle Oil decision is about labor and professions as much as item value.
Pigs reward a clean outdoor layout and a consistent collection habit.
Direct Truffle sales are often the better practical choice on busy farms, even when processing looks attractive on paper.
Plan around winter before depending on pigs as your main income source.
notes: Wiki/database source tier. Pig, Truffle, and processing mechanics are source-backed; yearly output and processing advantage remain farm-specific and should stay framed as estimates.

title: Fairy Rose Honey on Ginger Island: Low-Labor Artisan Income With Layout Discipline
summary: Use Fairy Rose Honey on Ginger Island as a low-labor layout route: protect the flower, place Bee Houses correctly, and expand only when crafting materials support it.
bestFor: Players with Ginger Island access who want recurring artisan income with low daily effort
Players who enjoy stable layout planning more than constant crop handling
Players with enough Bee House materials to scale gradually without starving other builds
whyItWorks: Bee Houses produce honey on a repeating cycle, and a valid nearby Fairy Rose changes the product into a much more valuable flavored honey.
Ginger Island reduces normal seasonal pressure, making a protected flower-and-Bee-House layout easier to maintain.
After construction, the route asks more for layout discipline than daily labor.
setupSteps: Unlock Ginger Island before treating this as a stable long-term income plan.
Plant Fairy Roses where they can remain in place and influence nearby Bee Houses.
Place Bee Houses within the valid flower range while leaving paths that make collection quick and mistake-resistant.
Do not harvest the Fairy Rose that flavors the setup unless you are intentionally ending or redesigning the layout.
Expand in batches that match your maple syrup, iron bar, coal, and wood supply.
Check the layout after changes so every Bee House still produces the intended honey instead of defaulting to Wild Honey.

profitEstimates.label: Per-honey reference
profitEstimates.assumption: Wiki-listed Bee House and Fairy Rose Honey values; profession and layout size are separate variables.
profitEstimates.value: Fairy Rose Honey has a much higher listed value than Wild Honey.

profitEstimates.label: Island layout model
profitEstimates.assumption: Derived route model: multiply working Bee Houses by completed collection cycles, then adjust for missed pickups, broken flower range, and layout downtime.
profitEstimates.value: Best treated as low-labor recurring income rather than a precise gold-per-day promise.

caveats: The route fails when the flower is harvested, moved, or outside the Bee House range.
Bee House materials compete with other machines and farm infrastructure.
Garden Pot and flower behavior changed in 1.6.6, so layout advice should stay version-aware.
Without Ginger Island or another valid long-term layout, the route is less convenient and more seasonal.
articleAngles: Fairy Rose Honey is a layout discipline route, not just a high-value item choice.
The main mistake is breaking the flower relationship that gives the honey its value.
Low labor does not mean no planning: paths, range, and collection cycles matter.
Expand the layout only when materials are not needed for higher-priority machines.
notes: Wiki/database source tier. Honey mechanics and Ginger Island relevance are source-backed; exact layouts, annual totals, and diagram-level claims remain derived and should be tested before publishing as fixed plans.

title: Fish Smoker Profit Route: Active Fishing Money in Stardew Valley 1.6
summary: Use the Fish Smoker when active fishing is already part of your day: spend coal on valuable catches, keep the machine running, and avoid treating every fish as worth smoking.
bestFor: Players who actively enjoy fishing and can catch valuable fish consistently
Players on Stardew Valley 1.6 or later with access to a Fish Smoker source
Players who can spare coal without slowing tool upgrades, smelting, or crafting
whyItWorks: The Fish Smoker converts fish plus coal into Smoked Fish and raises the sale value while preserving quality.
Because processing is short, it can fit into an active fishing day instead of waiting for a long artisan queue.
The route rewards fish selection, coal planning, and player skill more than passive farm layout.
setupSteps: Confirm that your save has access to the Fish Smoker through a valid recipe, starter item, or other in-game source.
Set aside coal before the fishing trip so the machine does not become the bottleneck.
Prioritize valuable fish first; do not automatically smoke low-value catches if coal is scarce.
Process fish while continuing other work so the short cycle supports the day instead of interrupting it.
Sell Smoked Fish through valid channels and remember that profession setup can affect the final value.
If the day produces weak catches, save coal for a better route instead of forcing the machine.

profitEstimates.label: Per-fish processing reference
profitEstimates.assumption: Wiki-listed Fish Smoker and Smoked Fish mechanics before coal opportunity cost, fish selection, and profession choices are applied.
profitEstimates.value: Smoked Fish sells for 2x fish price, or 2.8x with Artisan according to the wiki page.

profitEstimates.label: Active fishing day model
profitEstimates.assumption: Derived route estimate based on the number, quality, and value of fish caught in a day; player skill and fish selection dominate the result.
profitEstimates.value: Profitable when the catch value justifies coal and attention; weak if used on low-value fish or poor fishing days.

caveats: This is active income, not passive farm income; it depends on fishing skill and route choice.
Coal has real opportunity cost, especially early when smelting and crafting also need it.
The route is version-specific to 1.6 content and should not be assumed in older saves.
Daily profit varies heavily with fish availability, weather, bait, tackle, quality, and player execution.
articleAngles: The Fish Smoker rewards active players who can choose valuable targets and keep coal stocked.
Coal is the hidden cost, so smoke selectively instead of mechanically processing every catch.
The route is strongest when the machine supports a good fishing day, not when it tries to rescue a bad one.
Treat daily outcomes as variable until you test your own route.
notes: Wiki/database source tier. Fish Smoker mechanics are source-backed; daily route profit requires own testing because catch count, fish quality, weather, bait, tackle, and player skill vary heavily.

title: Preserves Jar vs Keg: How to Choose the Right Machine for Your Crops
summary: Choose Preserves Jars or Kegs by matching the crop, machine speed, and cash timing: jars help clear bulk crops sooner, while kegs support higher-value long-term processing.
bestFor: Players deciding which artisan machines to craft first
Players with crop stockpiles that are too large for their current machine count
Players comparing faster cashflow against slower high-value processing
whyItWorks: Preserves Jars use a formula that often helps lower-value bulk crops and clears product faster.
Kegs favor high-value fruit and long-term beverage routes where the multiplier and product type matter more.
The right answer depends on crop mix, machine count, and how soon the farm needs cash, not only on theoretical maximum value.
setupSteps: Separate crops into low-value bulk produce, high-value fruit, and items needed for quests or bundles before loading machines.
Use Preserves Jars when faster turnaround or low-value bulk processing solves your current cashflow problem.
Use Kegs for high-value fruit, wine plans, and routes where slower processing still fits your money timeline.
Ship some crops directly when waiting for perfect processing would delay a needed purchase.
Expand machines around the crops you repeatedly grow, not around a one-time harvest pile.
Recheck the decision when your field size, crop mix, and machine network change.

profitEstimates.label: Formula reference
profitEstimates.assumption: Wiki-listed Preserves Jar formula and Keg processing rules.
profitEstimates.value: Jellies and Pickles use 2x base crop value plus 50g; Kegs use different multipliers depending on product type.

profitEstimates.label: Backlog decision model
profitEstimates.assumption: Derived route advice: a technically higher-value product can still be worse for the farm if the queue delays cash too long.
profitEstimates.value: Choose the machine that clears your real crop volume at the timing your farm needs.

caveats: Crop quality usually does not carry into jar products, so compare against base crop value when using the formula.
One crop's preferred machine is not automatically the right machine for another crop.
Machine material cost, processing time, and available space can matter more than perfect spreadsheet value.
The route fails when all produce waits in a chest while the farm needed gold days ago.
articleAngles: Machine choice is a cashflow and backlog decision, not just a profit table.
Jars solve early bulk-crop pressure; kegs power slower high-value routes.
Use formulas as filters, then adjust for the machine count your farm actually has.
The best practical machine is the one that matches both the crop and the timing of your next purchase.
seoKeywords: Stardew Valley Preserves Jar vs Keg
notes: Wiki/database source tier. Machine formulas are source-backed; final recommendations are original decision guidance and should stay conditional by crop, machine count, material cost, and cash timing.
